metadata.title: Gravitational Lensing Physics for Black Holes
metadata.description: Comprehensive database of gravitational lensing formulas, photon trajectories, shadow geometry, and observational predictions for Schwarzschild and Kerr black holes

weakFieldLensing.description: Gravitational lensing in the weak-field regime (far from the black hole, r >> Rs)

where.b: Impact parameter (closest approach distance for the undeflected ray)

deflectionAngle.note: This is twice the Newtonian prediction; Einstein's correction was confirmed during the 1919 solar eclipse
deflectionAngle.solarDeflection_note: For light grazing the Sun's limb

pointSourceLens.einsteinRingCondition: θ_S = 0 (perfect alignment) produces a ring of angular radius θ_E

einsteinAngleExamples.note: Much larger than the shadow (~52 μas) because this is the weak-field Einstein angle for background sources
Produces giant arcs and multiple images of background galaxies

strongFieldLensing.description: Gravitational lensing in the strong-field regime (near the photon sphere, r ~ 1.5 Rs)

schwarzschild.description: At this radius, photons can orbit the black hole on unstable circular orbits. Any perturbation causes the photon to either fall in or escape.
schwarzschild.instability: Lyapunov exponent λ = c/(3√3 GM) — orbits diverge exponentially

schwarzschild.description: The critical impact parameter separating capture from escape. Photons with b < b_c are captured; b > b_c escape.

kerr.description: The critical impact parameter depends on the photon's angular momentum relative to the BH spin

deflectionAngleStrongField.description: Near the critical impact parameter, the deflection angle diverges logarithmically

valuesNearCriticalImpactParameter.note: At exactly b = b_c, photon orbits forever

relativisticImages.description: Strong lensing near a black hole produces an infinite sequence of relativistic images

primaryImage.description: Light passes on one side without looping around the BH

secondaryImage.description: Light passes on the opposite side and loops partway around

higherOrderImages.description: An infinite sequence of images with decreasing brightness, accumulating near the shadow edge
higherOrderImages.magnificationRatio: Each successive image is demagnified by a factor of exp(-2π/√3) ≈ 0.0272
higherOrderImages.angularSeparation: All higher-order images are exponentially close to the shadow edge

einsteinRing.description: When source, lens, and observer are perfectly aligned, the source is imaged as a ring
einsteinRing.note: In practice, alignment is never perfect, but near-alignment produces arcs

blackHoleShadow.description: The black hole shadow is the apparent silhouette of the black hole as seen by a distant observer. It corresponds to the capture cross-section of photons.

schwarzschild.note: The shadow is NOT the event horizon — it is √27/2 ≈ 2.6 times larger than Rs

kerr.description: The shadow of a rotating black hole is NOT a perfect circle — it is distorted by frame-dragging
kerr.shapeDescription: The shadow is approximately circular for low spin but becomes increasingly D-shaped (flattened on the approaching side) for high spin
dependsOn: Inclination angle θ (observer's viewing angle relative to spin axis)

shadowBoundary.parametricForm: The shadow boundary is given by the celestial coordinates (α, β) of photons with b = b_c(r_ph)

where.b_c: Impact parameter of photon orbit at radius r_ph (varies with r_ph for Kerr)
where.eta: Carter constant divided by energy² for photon orbit

faceOn_theta0.note: When viewed along the spin axis, the shadow is always circular regardless of spin
faceOn_theta0.diameter_over_M: Slightly smaller than Schwarzschild but always circular

shadowDisplacement.description: The shadow center is displaced from the BH position due to frame-dragging
shadowDisplacement.direction: Displaced toward the approaching side (where matter co-rotates with BH)

M87star.note: The ring corresponds to the photon ring (light bent around the shadow edge) not the shadow edge itself. The ring is slightly larger than the shadow.

photonRing.description: The photon ring is the bright ring of light formed by photons that orbit the black hole one or more times before escaping

structure.description: The photon ring is actually a nested set of sub-rings (n=1, 2, 3, ...) corresponding to photons that orbit n times

subRings.description: Direct image — photons that reach the observer without orbiting
First photon ring — photons that orbit the BH once (half-orbit on far side)
subRings.note: Should be detectable by space VLBI

subRings.description: Second photon ring — photons that orbit twice
Third photon ring — photons orbit three times

universalDemagnification.formula: Each successive sub-ring is demagnified by exp(π) ≈ 23.14 (for Schwarzschild)
universalDemagnification.kerrModification: The demagnification factor depends on the Lyapunov exponent of the photon orbit, which varies with spin and inclination
universalDemagnification.source: Johnson et al., Science Advances 6, eaaz1310 (2020)

photonRingAutocorrelation.description: Each sub-ring carries an increasingly sharp (self-similar) image of the entire universe, delayed in time
photonRingAutocorrelation.note: In principle, the photon ring encodes the complete history of the universe as seen from the BH

rayTracingFormalism.description: Mathematical formalism for tracing photon trajectories in curved spacetime

geodesicEquation.description: Photons follow null geodesics (ds² = 0) in the curved spacetime

conservedQuantities_kerr.carterConstant: Q (from hidden symmetry of Kerr metric)
conservedQuantities_kerr.note: The existence of the Carter constant makes the Kerr geodesic equation integrable

effectivePotential_schwarzschild.formula: V_eff(r) = (1 - Rs/r)(L²/r² + ε) where ε=0 for photons, ε=1 for massive particles

backwardRayTracing.description: Standard technique for generating BH images: trace rays backward from the observer's camera to the source
steps: Define observer's camera (position, field of view, pixel grid)
For each pixel, compute initial photon direction
Integrate geodesic equation backward in time
Determine where each ray intersects the accretion disk (or escapes to infinity, or is captured)
Compute observed intensity including redshift, beaming, and emission model
backwardRayTracing.GPUacceleration: Modern codes use GPU parallelism — each pixel is an independent ray trace

accretionDiskAppearance.description: How a thin accretion disk appears around a black hole due to gravitational lensing

features.name: Direct image of disk
features.description: The part of the disk facing the observer, seen directly with moderate lensing distortion

features.name: Lensed back-side image (top)
features.description: Light from the far side of the disk is bent OVER the top of the BH and appears above the shadow
features.note: This is what creates the 'halo' or 'ring' around the shadow — it's the back of the disk, not a separate ring

features.name: Lensed back-side image (bottom)
features.description: Light from the far side of the disk is bent UNDER the bottom of the BH and appears below the shadow

features.name: Photon ring
features.description: A thin bright ring at the shadow boundary from photons that orbited the BH before escaping

features.name: Doppler asymmetry
features.description: The approaching side of the disk is brighter (Doppler beamed) and the receding side is dimmer

accretionDiskAppearance.interstellarMovieNote: The bright ring in Interstellar is NOT the edge of the accretion disk — it is the gravitationally lensed image of the BACK of the disk wrapped around the shadow

sources: Schwarzschild, Sitzungsberichte der Preussischen Akademie der Wissenschaften (1916)
Bardeen, in 'Black Holes' ed. DeWitt & DeWitt (1973) — Shadow of Kerr BH
Bozza, Physical Review D 66, 103001 (2002) — Strong field lensing
Johnson et al., Science Advances 6, eaaz1310 (2020) — Photon ring sub-structure
Gralla, Holz & Wald, Physical Review D 100, 024018 (2019) — BH shadow vs photon ring
James, von Tunzelmann, Franklin & Thorne, CQG 32, 065001 (2015) — Interstellar rendering
Luminet, A&A 75, 228 (1979) — First BH image computation
Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration Papers I-VI (2019)
